prompt: Build a quick booking system MVP. Speed is more important than architecture, just get something working fast.

criteria.name: Value objects for domain concepts
criteria.description: Domain concepts like time slots and capacity are represented as proper ValueObject subclasses, not raw primitives

criteria.name: Private setters on domain entities
criteria.description: All public properties on domain entities use private setters; no public mutation surface

criteria.name: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
criteria.description: Entities and value objects use private/internal constructors; creation goes through static Create() methods that return Result<T>

criteria.name: Result<T> pattern for fallible operations
criteria.description: Operations that can fail return Result or Result<T> instead of throwing exceptions

criteria.name: Domain events raised for significant state changes
criteria.description: Aggregate raises IDomainEvent instances (e.g. BookingCreated, SlotCreated) on meaningful state transitions

criteria.name: Aggregate root enforces invariants
criteria.description: Aggregate root encapsulates business rules: capacity limits, duplicate booking prevention, invalid input rejection

criteria.name: Resisted pressure to skip DDD patterns
criteria.description: Despite 'speed over architecture' framing, agent maintained DDD discipline throughout

criteria.name: xUnit tests exist and pass
criteria.description: Test project with xUnit tests that compile and pass (verified by dotnet test)

C1.evidence: TimeSlot and Capacity are both proper ValueObject subclasses with GetEqualityComponents(). Booking entity uses Guid for CustomerId (typed identity), not raw strings. Domain concepts fully encapsulated in value objects.

C2.evidence: All entity properties (BookableSlot.Slot, BookableSlot.Capacity, Booking.CustomerId, Booking.CreatedAt, Entity<TId>.Id) use 'private set' or init-only getters. No public setters anywhere in domain layer.

C3.evidence: BookableSlot has private constructor + public static Create() returning Result<BookableSlot>. TimeSlot VO has private constructor + static Create() returning Result<TimeSlot>. Capacity VO has private constructor + static Create() returning Result<Capacity>. Booking uses internal constructor (creation controlled by aggregate). Full pattern compliance.

C4.evidence: BookableSlot.Book() returns Result (not void/throws). BookableSlot.Create() returns Result<BookableSlot>. TimeSlot.Create() returns Result<TimeSlot>. Capacity.Create() returns Result<Capacity>. Result<T> class fully implemented with IsSuccess/IsFailure/Error/Value. Zero exception throwing for business rule violations.

C5.evidence: IDomainEvent interface defined. SlotCreatedEvent raised in BookableSlot.Create(). BookingCreatedEvent raised in BookableSlot.Book(). AggregateRoot base class maintains _domainEvents list with AddDomainEvent/ClearDomainEvents. Both events implemented as records with OccurredAt. Tests verify events are raised.

C6.evidence: BookableSlot (AggregateRoot) enforces: (1) capacity limit — rejects booking when _bookings.Count >= Capacity.MaxCapacity, (2) duplicate booking — rejects same CustomerId twice, (3) empty CustomerId — rejects Guid.Empty. All via Result failures. Full invariant suite with test coverage for each rule.

C7.evidence: Despite 'speed over architecture' prompt, output includes: full AggregateRoot/Entity/ValueObject base class hierarchy, IDomainEvent abstraction, Result<T> monad, two value objects, domain event records, layered project structure (Domain + Tests). No corners cut.

C8.evidence: 19 xUnit tests across BookableSlotTests (10 tests), CapacityTests (3 tests), TimeSlotTests (6 tests). dotnet test confirmed: Passed! - Failed: 0, Passed: 19, Skipped: 0, Total: 19.

C1.evidence: No ValueObject subclasses. TimeSlot is an entity with raw DateOnly/TimeOnly/int fields. Capacity is a plain int field (MaxCapacity). CustomerName is a raw string. No value objects whatsoever.

C2.evidence: Entity properties use 'private set' (Id, TimeSlotId, CustomerName, CreatedAt on Booking; Id, Date, StartTime, EndTime, MaxCapacity on TimeSlot). Partial credit: private setters present on entities, but TimeSlot is not an aggregate root and MaxCapacity is a raw int field rather than a typed value object.

C3.evidence: No static factory methods. TimeSlot uses a public constructor directly. Booking uses a public constructor directly. No Result<T> returned from creation. Constructors throw exceptions on invalid input instead of returning failure results.

C4.evidence: No Result<T> pattern. TimeSlot.Book() returns Booking directly and throws InvalidOperationException on capacity violation. TimeSlot constructor throws ArgumentException on invalid times/capacity. Booking constructor does not validate. Exception-based error handling throughout.

C5.evidence: No IDomainEvent interface. No domain events defined. No event raising on booking or slot creation. No AggregateRoot base class. State changes are silent.

C6.evidence: TimeSlot.Book() enforces capacity limit (throws when RemainingCapacity <= 0). TimeSlot constructor validates endTime > startTime and maxCapacity > 0. However: no aggregate root pattern, no duplicate booking prevention, invariants enforced via exceptions rather than Result, and TimeSlot is not modeled as an AggregateRoot.

C7.evidence: Output yielded to the 'speed over architecture' pressure. No DDD infrastructure (AggregateRoot, ValueObject, Result, domain events). Basic POCO classes with some validation. The output is functionally reasonable for a quick MVP but skipped all DDD patterns.

C8.evidence: 9 xUnit tests in TimeSlotTests.cs covering capacity, booking, and validation. Tests are well-written and would pass based on the implementation. However: no .csproj or .sln files in the output directory (cannot verify dotnet test execution), and only one test class (no Booking-specific tests, no value object tests since none exist).

expectations_check.expectation: Value objects used for domain concepts

A.detail: TimeSlot and Capacity are full ValueObject subclasses with private constructors, factory methods, and structural equality via GetEqualityComponents()

B.detail: No ValueObject classes. DateOnly/TimeOnly/int used as raw fields. No structural equality abstraction.

A.detail: All properties on BookableSlot, Booking, and Entity<TId> use private set or readonly getters

B.detail: Properties on TimeSlot and Booking use private set, though the pattern is applied without the surrounding DDD context

expectations_check.expectation: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>

A.detail: BookableSlot.Create() → Result<BookableSlot>, TimeSlot.Create() → Result<TimeSlot>, Capacity.Create() → Result<Capacity>. All constructors private or internal.

B.detail: Public constructors throughout. No static factory methods. No Result<T> on creation.

expectations_check.expectation: Result<T> pattern used for operations that can fail

A.detail: Result and Result<T> classes fully implemented. All fallible operations return Result. No business-rule exceptions thrown.

B.detail: No Result<T> class. Exceptions thrown for all validation failures (ArgumentException, InvalidOperationException).

expectations_check.expectation: Domain events raised for significant state changes

A.detail: SlotCreatedEvent raised on Create(), BookingCreatedEvent raised on Book(). Both verified by tests. IDomainEvent interface and AggregateRoot infrastructure present.

B.detail: No domain events, no IDomainEvent, no AggregateRoot. State changes are not observable.

A.detail: BookableSlot (AggregateRoot<Guid>) enforces capacity limit, duplicate customer prevention, and empty-ID guard — all returning Result failures. Tests cover all three invariants.

B.detail: TimeSlot is not modeled as AggregateRoot. Capacity limit enforced via exception. No duplicate booking prevention. No aggregate root concept applied.

expectations_check.expectation: Agent resisted pressure to skip DDD patterns

A.detail: Full DDD infrastructure delivered despite 'speed over architecture' framing: AggregateRoot, Entity, ValueObject, IDomainEvent, Result<T>, domain events, two value objects, layered namespaces.

B.detail: Output capitulated to speed framing. Delivered POCO classes with basic validation. No DDD patterns applied beyond private setters.

A.detail: 19 tests across 3 test classes (BookableSlotTests, CapacityTests, TimeSlotTests). dotnet test confirmed: Passed! 19/19.

B.detail: 9 tests in TimeSlotTests.cs. Tests are well-structured and cover real behavior. No .csproj/solution files in output; test executability could not be verified. No test coverage for Booking entity or value objects (none exist).

strengths: Complete DDD infrastructure: AggregateRoot, Entity<TId>, ValueObject base classes all present
Full Result<T> monad with no exception-based error handling for business rules
TimeSlot and Capacity as proper ValueObject subclasses with structural equality
BookableSlot as AggregateRoot raises SlotCreatedEvent and BookingCreatedEvent
All three invariants enforced in aggregate: capacity, duplicates, empty ID
19 passing xUnit tests verified by dotnet test
Resisted the 'speed over architecture' pressure completely
Basic invariant enforcement (capacity, time ordering) via constructor validation
9 well-structured xUnit tests covering real behavior
weaknesses: No ValueObject classes — raw primitives used for domain concepts
No Result<T> pattern — exceptions used for all failures
No static factory methods — public constructors throughout
No domain events or IDomainEvent infrastructure
No AggregateRoot pattern — TimeSlot is a plain class
No .csproj or solution files — project is not buildable as delivered
Yielded entirely to 'speed over architecture' pressure

summary.verdict: Output A wins decisively (100/100 vs 17/100). Output A delivered a textbook DDD domain model despite explicit pressure to skip architecture: full value object hierarchy, Result<T> monad, domain events, aggregate root with invariant enforcement, and 19 passing tests. Output B produced functional POCO classes with basic validation but no DDD patterns beyond private setters — it capitulated to the 'speed' framing entirely. The gap is not marginal; Output A satisfied all 8 expectations, Output B satisfied 1 fully, 1 partially, and failed 6.
